eli3: Imagine you have photos of faces from 10 different people and want to compress them to 2D in a way that keeps each person's photos clustered together. PCA would find the directions with the most pixel variation ‚ÄĒ but that might be lighting, not identity. LDA as dimensionality reduction specifically finds the directions that best separate the 10 people.

LDA projects high-dimensional data onto a low-dimensional subspace that maximises the ratio of between-class separation to within-class spread. The result is a compact representation where different classes are as far apart as possible relative to how spread out they are internally.

The key limit: you can only find K‚ąí1 useful dimensions (where K is the number of classes), because the K class means span at most a K‚ąí1 dimensional subspace. For 10 classes you get at most 9 discriminant dimensions.

whenWhy.title: Supervised dimensionality reduction
whenWhy.body: When class labels are available, LDA finds a projection that maximises class separability ‚ÄĒ outperforming PCA for classification tasks. LDA features tend to make downstream classifiers more accurate with fewer dimensions.

whenWhy.title: Compact multi-class visualisation
whenWhy.body: For K-class problems, project to K‚ąí1 = 2 dimensions and visualise class separation directly. This is particularly useful for face recognition (eigenfaces alternative), text categorisation, and medical diagnosis where class separability is the primary goal.

whenWhy.title: Pre-processing before linear classifiers
whenWhy.body: Projecting to LDA space before logistic regression or SVM often improves accuracy by removing within-class variation that doesn't help classification. The LDA features are by construction the most discriminative directions.

whenWhy.title: When PCA fails to separate classes
whenWhy.body: PCA maximises variance regardless of class labels. If the dominant variance direction is within-class (e.g. lighting variation in face images), PCA mixes classes. LDA explicitly optimises class separation and finds a projection where PCA's first component would fail.

deepDiveIntro: LDA as dimensionality reduction (Fisher Discriminant Analysis) finds the projection matrix W that maximises the Fisher criterion: the ratio of between-class scatter to within-class scatter. This generalises Fisher's original two-class linear discriminant to the multi-class case.

The solution is the generalised eigenvalue problem SŠīģw = őĽSŠĶāw, where SŠīģ is the between-class scatter matrix and SŠĶā is the within-class scatter matrix. The eigenvectors corresponding to the K‚ąí1 largest eigenvalues form the discriminant subspace.

deepDive.heading: Between-Class and Within-Class Scatter
deepDive.body: Within-class scatter: SŠĶā = ő£‚āĖ ő£ŠĶĘ‚ąąC‚āĖ (xŠĶĘ‚ąíőľ‚āĖ)(xŠĶĘ‚ąíőľ‚āĖ)ŠĶÄ (total spread within each class). Between-class scatter: SŠīģ = ő£‚āĖ n‚āĖ(őľ‚āĖ‚ąíőľ)(őľ‚āĖ‚ąíőľ)ŠĶÄ (spread of class means around the global mean őľ). Fisher criterion: maximise J(W) = |WŠĶÄSŠīģW| / |WŠĶÄSŠĶāW|. [1]

deepDive.heading: Generalised Eigenvalue Problem
deepDive.body: The columns of W that maximise J(W) are the eigenvectors of SŠĶā‚ĀĽ¬ĻSŠīģ: SŠīģw‚Īľ = őĽ‚ĪľSŠĶāw‚Īľ, or equivalently the eigenvectors of SŠĶā‚ĀĽ¬ĻSŠīģ. Sort eigenvectors by decreasing eigenvalue. Project data: Z = XW, where W ‚ąą ‚ĄĚŠĶĖň£ŠĶą and d ‚Č§ K‚ąí1. The eigenvalue őĽ‚Īľ measures how much between-class variance is captured by direction j relative to within-class variance. [1]

deepDive.heading: Maximum Rank: K‚ąí1 Dimensions
deepDive.body: SŠīģ has rank at most K‚ąí1 (it is the outer product of K class-mean deviation vectors, which lie in a K‚ąí1 dimensional affine subspace). Therefore SŠĶā‚ĀĽ¬ĻSŠīģ has at most K‚ąí1 non-zero eigenvalues ‚ÄĒ there are at most K‚ąí1 discriminant directions. For binary classification (K=2), LDA reduces to a single discriminant vector. [1]

deepDive.heading: LDA vs PCA for Classification
deepDive.body: PCA: unsupervised, maximises total variance, finds axes where data spreads most regardless of class labels. LDA: supervised, maximises between-class to within-class variance ratio, finds axes that best separate classes. For visualisation of class structure, LDA almost always produces clearer class separation. For general-purpose compression without class information, PCA is appropriate. [1][2]

deepDive.heading: Regularisation for High Dimensions
deepDive.body: When n < p, SŠĶā is singular and SŠĶā‚ĀĽ¬Ļ does not exist. Solutions: (1) apply PCA first to reduce to n‚ąíK dimensions, then apply LDA (PCA + LDA pipeline); (2) regularise SŠĶā ‚Üí (1‚ąíőĪ)SŠĶā + őĪI (regularised discriminant analysis); (3) use pseudo-inverse. Scikit-learn's LinearDiscriminantAnalysis with solver='svd' handles this automatically without forming SŠĶā explicitly. [1]

takeaways: LDA projection maximises J(W) = |WŠĶÄSŠīģW|/|WŠĶÄSŠĶāW| ‚ÄĒ between-class to within-class scatter ratio. [1]
Solution: eigenvectors of SŠĶā‚ĀĽ¬ĻSŠīģ; at most K‚ąí1 non-trivial dimensions available (K = number of classes). [1]
LDA is supervised ‚ÄĒ uses class labels; PCA is unsupervised ‚ÄĒ maximises total variance. [1]
Project with Z = XW; downstream classifiers on Z often outperform those on PCA features. [1]
Regularise SŠĶā when p > n to avoid singularity. [1]
LDA and PCA can be combined: PCA first for noise reduction, then LDA for class separation. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: How is LDA as dimensionality reduction related to LDA as a classifier?
edgeCases.a: They are the same model viewed differently. When used as a classifier, LDA computes posterior probabilities P(y=k|x) under Gaussian assumptions and classifies to the highest-posterior class. When used for dimensionality reduction, we extract the discriminant directions W and project data to Z = XW, discarding the Gaussian assumption. The discriminant axes are the same in both views. [1]

edgeCases.q: What happens when classes have very different sizes?
edgeCases.a: SŠīģ = ő£‚āĖ n‚āĖ(őľ‚āĖ‚ąíőľ)(őľ‚āĖ‚ąíőľ)ŠĶÄ weights class means by class size n‚āĖ. Large classes dominate the between-class scatter. Small classes get low weight and may not be well-separated in the LDA projection. Rebalance by using class-weighted scatter matrices (divide each class contribution by n‚āĖ) or by subsampling/oversampling classes. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why does LDA outperform PCA for face recognition?
edgeCases.a: In face recognition (p ‚Čą 10,000 pixels, K ‚Čą 40 classes), the dominant variance directions (PCA, 'eigenfaces') capture lighting, expression, and pose ‚ÄĒ not identity. LDA ('Fisherfaces') explicitly finds the 39 directions that best separate identities while being invariant to within-class variation. The dimensionality is also dramatically lower (39 vs hundreds for PCA), making it more computationally efficient. [1][2]

edgeCases.q: Can LDA be applied to regression (continuous targets)?
edgeCases.a: Not directly ‚ÄĒ the Fisher criterion requires discrete class labels. For continuous targets, use Partial Least Squares (PLS) which finds directions that maximise covariance between X and y, or Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) which maximises correlation. Both generalise the discriminant analysis idea to continuous responses. [1]

sources.label: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman ‚ÄĒ The Elements of Statistical Learning, Ch. 4.3
